data.title: Viper Energy: Competitive Advantages Get Better Through The Sitio Royalties Acquisition
data.description:
Thesis

On June 3rd, Viper Energy (NASDAQ:VNOM) announced the acquisition of Sitio Royalties (NYSE:STR) in an all stock deal. The transaction valued STR at $4.1 billion or $19.41/share, a healthy premium to the $17.32/share closing price on June 2nd.

Despite the premium, the deal remains accretive for VNOM shareholders. As part of the announcement, VNOM increased the base dividend by 10% to an annual payment of $1.32/share. The financial footing of the company was also improved with the dividend now covered as low as $20/barrel WTI. This is a $2/barrel improvement.

Beyond the headline numbers, the deal gives VNOM an even larger competitive advantage by increasing the scale of acreage exposed to its parent company Diamondback Energy (FANG). The unique relationship with FANG gives VNOM a voice at the table when drilling plans are made while also keeping G&A costs low, one of the largest expenses for a royalty company.

I expect much of the overhead costs at STR to be eliminated in the longer term, enhancing VNOM's profit margins in the coming years. This additional lever for value enhancement provides another dimension to the value add proposition of the deal.

I rate VNOM as a BUY following the expansion of its competitive advantage by acquiring Sitio Royalties.

Terms Of The Deal

Shareholders of STR will be granted VNOM's class A shares on a 0.4855:1 basis. VNOM calculates that this translates into a share price of $19.41/share, slightly more than a $2/share premium for Sitio's stock.

The deal immediately improved VNOM's performance on a produced oil per share basis. The improvement in this metric allowed the company to simultaneously increase the dividend by 10% to $1.32/share, while also reducing the breakeven level to $20/barrel WTI.

The improved scale of the transaction is also coupled with improved durability through the acquired Sitio acreage. The STR portfolio included over 26,000 actively producing wells compared to just 15,300 in the VNOM portfolio.

While the vast majority of these wells are likely well past their peak productions years, it significantly expands the company's low decline production base. In doing so, a larger percentage of the company's total production comes from "stable" sources, and less dependant on new well activity.

A Competitive Advantage That Just Got Bigger

As a royalty company, Viper Energy has an unique advantage by being a subsidiary of Diamondback Energy. Typically, one of the drawbacks of the royalty business model is the lack of control on future production. Drillers have to be incentivized to drill on an owner's acreage.

However in the case of FANG, the parent company can realize improved economics by drilling on VNOM's acreage. FANG's 41% ownership of the proforma company essentially becomes a pass through mechanism for the company's royalty expenses. In return, VNOM enjoys a competitive advantage to incentivize drilling on its Permian acreage.

As shown below, the Sitio acreage fits nicely into the existing Viper territory. It is also worth noting that FANG represented 5.5% of STR's total production and was the company's 7th largest producer prior to the merger.

Initially, this 5.5% figure is expected to be dilutive in nature to the company's exposure to FANG's activity. The combined company will generate roughly 42% of its production from FANG, down from 54% in 2024. However, VNOM expects the additional runway of advantaged acreage will allow Diamondback to increase its total production to over 45% by 2026. I believe this clearly demonstrates the economic benefits that FANG is capable of realizing by drilling on VNOM land.

The Benefits Of Scale

One of the key differences between VNOM and STR is its cost of management. Since royalty companies have no tangible production costs, the bulk of the expenses are usually in the form of interest and G&A. Typically with scale, one would expect there to be efficiency in the company's management costs.

However, the chart below shows two distinct differences between STR and VNOM. First, STR's management fees were quite high when compared to peers including Kimbell Royalty (KRP) and Black Stone Minerals (BSM). Second, G&A for STR was approximately 9% of total revenues compared to just 2% for VNOM.

Clearly there is a lot of meat on the bone here as the management team of STR is likely to be allowed to find "new opportunities". VNOM announced that this would be the primary source of synergies in the transaction, which were expected to amount to over $50 million.

Valuation

In this transaction, VNOM was able to roll up one of the cheaper options in the royalty space. STR traded at roughly 8x EBITDA, nearly a full turn lower than VNOM and KRP. The $2 premium paid for STR's shares brings the company generally in line with VNOM itself. Therefore, even after the premium it looks like VNOM got a fair deal here.

Moving forward, the deal's value will be enhanced through EBITDA gains related to the previously mentioned G&A savings. In Q1, STR spent $15.8 million in G&A against a total EBITDA of $142 million. The 10% improvement to STR's EBITDA through G&A savings makes this an attractive deal in own right.

Risks

The royalty sector is naturally linked to changes in commodity prices. The proforma company has provided an estimated range of FCF yields across a wide spread of WTI prices. As oil prices fluctuate, so does the financial performance of an investment in VNOM.

As I previously discussed on my coverage of FANG in May, I expect crude prices to be firmly locked in between $60-$65/barrel. This is largely predicated on the aggressive moves by OPEC+ to absorb most (if not all) of global demand growth through 2026.

However the pace of those production increases seems to change daily. Should OPEC+ continue to mash on the accelerator, oil prices maybe pushed downward toward $55-$60/barrel. In this range, investors will likely sacrifice some yield as the investor payout moves into the 6.4%-6.9%.

I believe at this level VNOM's risks actually drop off rather meaningfully. First, this is where VNOM's hedges begin to kick in. The company had previously hedged 20,000 bbl/d of production at $55/barrel. Second, at this level activity from FANG is likely to increase as the savings generated by drilling on VNOM's acreage helps to preserve margins for the parent company.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. The acquisition of STR give VNOM the ability to lean further into its own strengths. Having a higher exposure to FANG's operations gives VNOM more influence over drilling activity. The lack of control is typically one of the major drawbacks to investing in the royalty space.

2. The deal represents an opportunity to improve the high spend G&A in the STR portfolio. Sitio's management spent roughly 9% of last years revenues on operating the company. I expect VNOM will beat its announced $50 million in synergy savings.

3. STR had previously traded at a discount to its peers, trading around 1.5 turns lower than VNOM on an EV:EBITDA basis. The premium paid for STR largely negates this discount. Overall, I see the transaction as fair for both sides.

4. VNOM expects to realize production growth through its relationship with Diamondback Energy. In the near term, VNOM will likely be an outlier in total production as much of the rest of the industry pauses on growth. This exemplifies the competitive advantage possessed by VNOM.

data.title: Thornburg International Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

With headlines dominated by inflation concerns, recession fears, and tariff tensions, it is no surprise investors are feeling uneasy. Uncertainty causes doubt. Yet, it is precisely in these moments of uncertainty that opportunities emerge, especially in international markets in 2025.

For the past decade and a half, U.S. equities have taken center stage. But now, international markets—often underappreciated—are revealing compelling potential. As the U.S. contends with persistent inflation and ebbing growth, China faces deflation and plans massive stimulus, Japan moves beyond a decades-long deflationary environment, and Europe begins to assert greater economic independence, international markets offer a diverse set of investment conditions worth exploring.

While tariffs remain a topical issue for the U.S., some regions are more affected than others. It is important not to project the U.S. experience onto other economies—each operates under its own unique set of circumstances.

Market swings can be unsettling, but volatility is not inherently negative. For long-term investors, it can be a powerful ally. Volatility creates windows of opportunity—periods when patience, discipline, and a long-term mindset can pave the way for positive outcomes. During the recent bouts of market turbulence, we have been actively capitalizing on select opportunities.

Every economic cloud has a silver lining. Our team is here to help clients navigate through uncertain waters—by integrating conviction in multi-year growth themes with rigorous fundamental analysis and disciplined portfolio management.

Current Positioning and Outlook

U.S. tariff-induced market volatility reflected growing concerns about the impact on global economic growth, exacerbating recent doubts about the return on investment in the infrastructure buildout necessary for the widespread industry adoption of AI. Continued pressure on the shares of one of our top pharma holdings also undercut portfolio performance in the first quarter. We retain conviction in the long-term prospects of our holdings, which we consider well positioned to benefit from technological innovation and long-term demographic trends.

We have observed a disconnect between coincident indicators in recent corporate earnings and share price performance. March revenue at the world’s top semiconductor manufacturer was up nearly 50% from March 2024 and rose just more than 40% from a year earlier in the first quarter of 2024. The world’s largest hyperscalers have reaffirmed over the last three months their massive capex budgets for data center expansions. Yet this and other stocks that directly benefit from the AI buildout saw their share prices decline, as the market doubts the sustainability of growth.

Declining valuations in AI’s enabling technologies, combined with robust fundamental demand as adoption expands across businesses and consumers, only reinforces our conviction in the transformative potential of this technology and the equity returns that should follow.

data.title: Chinese Banks' NIMs Near 'Critical Point'
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome SX Invest as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The current valuation level of Chinese commercial bank stocks is significantly lower than the average level of international peers. At the end of May, the 2024 P/B of Chinese bank ADRs was as low as around 0.4x-0.5x. In comparison, HSBC (HSBC) was 1.1x, Citi (C) was 0.72x, Bank of America (BAC) was 1.2x, and Wells Fargo (WFC) was 1.5x. The low valuation level reflects investors' continued concerns about the decline in NIMs of Chinese commercial banks in the past few years.

However, based on my analysis, I believe that there is not much room for the NIM to decline. The decline of about 10-11bp in 2025 is slightly better than the market consensus at the beginning of the year. This may provide a cyclical buying point for Chinese bank stocks, especially when the NIM moves closer to the theoretical critical point of 1.27%.

Since the LPR reform, multiple factors have led to a continuous decline in net interest margin

Since the LPR reform in August 2019, the net interest margin (NIM) continued to decline. In 2025Q1, the net interest margin of commercial banks had dropped by 77 bp to 1.43 compared with the end of 2019. In retrospect, the trend of net interest margin since 2020 can be roughly divided into four stages: the rapid downward stage under the impact of the epidemic (2020), the low-level shock stage of economic recovery and policy support (2021), the accelerated downward stage under multiple pressures (2022-2023Q1), and the marginal stabilization stage with a narrowing (2023Q2-2024Q4).

I believe that there are several main reasons for the decline in net interest margin:

1. The difference in the pricing mechanism of deposit and loan interest rates leads to the natural pressure of narrowing the interest rate spread. The pace of loan repricing is faster. The loan repricing cycle refers to the time interval for the interest rate agreed in the loan contract to be adjusted according to the pricing benchmark (LPR), which can be three months, six months or one year. In addition to fixed-rate loans that lock in interest rates during the term, most loans linked to LPR choose one year as the repricing cycle.

Deposit repricing is relatively sticky. There is no repricing for deposits themselves. The deposit repricing often talked about mostly refers to the renewal of deposits upon maturity. Because of the attribute of being withdrawn at any time, demand deposits can be understood as being able to be repriced at any time, but demand deposits accounted for only 20% at the end of April 2025. The main time deposit terms include 3M/6M/1Y/2Y/3Y/5Y, and the repricing cycle is the corresponding deposit period. Taking the data of the six state-owned banks in 2024 as an example, deposits within 3M/3M-1Y/1-5Y/5Y and above accounted for 25.8%/35.4%/38.7%/0.1% of time deposits respectively. Setting the duration to the median of each term range, the weighted average duration of time deposits is 1.42 years, which is significantly higher than the 1-year loan repricing cycle.

In short, by combing through the pricing mechanism of deposits and loans, it is evident that due to the differences in pricing mechanisms and the asynchronous pace of interest rate marketization reform, it is difficult for loan and deposit pricing to change in the same direction in equal proportion. Regardless of whether the central bank cuts interest rates or not, the downward speed of loan yields is often significantly faster than that of deposits, which leads to natural pressure to narrow bank interest margins.

2. Various profit-sharing measures have pushed loan interest rates down. Since 2020, Chinese regulators have introduced a series of measures to guide banks to give up profits to the real economy, including LPR rate cuts, MPA loan interest rate spread assessments, the creation and high-frequency use of re-lending tools (the interest rate has been lowered to 1.5%, previously 1.75% for a long time, consistent with the financial stability re-lending rate), and reductions in existing mortgage rates, which have led to a continuous decline in loan interest rates.

3. Insufficient effective demand leads to spontaneous downward pressure on loan pricing. Since the Covid-19 outbreak, in order to further support the real economy, the central bank has repeatedly instructed banks to increase credit supply, but the growth of effective credit demand has continued to slow. The year-on-year growth rate of medium- and long-term loans at the end of 2024 has dropped by 6.1 percentage points to 7.8% from the end of 2019. In contrast, the proportion of loans priced at the LPR reduction in December 2024 was 44.9%, a sharp increase of 23 percentage points from December 2019. This shows that even if the LPR is not lowered, the contradiction between supply and demand will cause a spontaneous reduction in the loan interest rate spread, which will lead to a decline in the actual implementation rate.

4. Structural changes such as the obstruction of retail loan issuance and the strengthening of the trend of regular deposits and long-term deposits squeeze the NIM. From the point of view of bank product pricing, retail loans have the highest yield, and demand deposits have the lowest interest rate. Taking ICBC as an example, the average yield of retail loans in 2024 can still reach 3.65%, while the average personal current interest rate is only 0.17%. As the gap gradually widens, such structural changes also squeeze the NIM from both sides of the balance sheet.

Current interest rate margin rigidity bottom line may be 0.67%, and the critical point of NIM, 1.27%

In the established regulatory policy framework, the "Eligible Prudential Assessment (EPA)" puts forward clear quantitative requirements for bank interest margins. In the EPA assessment, the interest margin score is divided into three grades. Specific scoring criteria: NIM ≥ 1.8%, 100 points; 0.8% ≤ NIM < 1.8%, 60-100 points; NIM < 0.8%, 0 point. Average bank interest margin of 2023Q1 dropped to 1.74%, entering the second level of 0.8-1.8%, and in 2025Q1 it further declined to 1.43%. This means that many banks scored 60-100 points, although they still meet the EPA assessment requirements and will not face the risk of "downgrading" for the time being.

In the 2023Q2 Monetary Policy Implementation Report, the Chinese central bank clearly stated that "commercial banks need to maintain a reasonable level of profit and net interest margin to maintain sound operation and prevent financial risks, which is also conducive to enhancing the sustainability of commercial banks in supporting the real economy". This statement shows that regulators have realized the need to protect the reasonable net interest margin of banks. From 2023Q2, debt cost mitigation measures have become more frequent, the net interest margin of banks has also entered the stage of marginal stabilization.

According to my calculation, if NIM falls below 0.67%, China's commercial banks will fall into industry-wide losses. From the historical data, regulators generally maintain at least 60bp safety cushion between the actual NIM and the theoretical lowest point. If the safety cushion is less than 60 BP, it is likely to trigger unconventional policy adjustments. Therefore, I judge that current critical point of NIM in China's banking industry is around 1.27%, which is only 16bp lower than the level at the end of the 2025Q1.

NIM is expected to decline by about 10-11 bp throughout the year of 2025

I expect that the NIM will continue to show a slowing downward trend in 2025, and will show an "L-shaped" trend with a significant decline in the first quarter, a slight decline in the last three quarters, or even stabilization. Specifically:

1. The repricing of existing loans in the first quarter is expected to drag down the interest rate spread by about 14bp. The 5-year LPR in 2024 has fallen by 60bp cumulatively, mainly affecting the interest rate spread in 2025 through the repricing in 2025Q1. According to the term structure of listed banks that are repriced or expired according to contracts at the end of 2024, the proportions of less than 3M/3M-1Y/1-5Y/5Y and above are 44%/47%/6%/2%, respectively, and the term structure of listed banks' loan maturity at the end of 2024, the proportions of less than 3M/3M-1Y/1-5Y/5Y and above are 13%/25%/25%/36%, respectively. Therefore, assuming that the proportion of repriced loans in the first quarter is 32%, the interest income is expected to decrease by 485.8 billion in 2025Q1, and the NIM will decrease by about 14bp.

2. The pricing of newly issued loans has spontaneously declined, which is expected to drag down the interest rate spread by about 4bp. The loan execution rate is formed by adding or subtracting points on the basis of LPR. In recent years, the continued decline in the interest rate of newly issued loans, in addition to the impact of the LPR reduction, the role of the imbalance between credit supply and demand cannot be ignored. Assuming that the proportion of loans priced with reference to the 1-year LPR and the 5-year LPR is 20% and 80% respectively, I calculate the weighted average LPR from the end of 2019 to the end of 2025Q1, and subtract it from the weighted average interest rate of newly issued general loans. The difference is the "spread" of the pricing of newly issued loans. It can be seen that the spread has been shrinking in recent years . The spread at the end of 2025Q1 has shrunk from 82bp to 25bp compared with the end of 2019. I further calculated the spread reductions in 2020-2024, which were 26bp, 10bp, 31bp, 10bp, and -2bp respectively. After the spread was greatly reduced in 2022, the reduction in 2023 has narrowed, and even returned to the rising range in 2024. Considering that the spread trend has entered a downward slowdown stage since 2023, and the central bank has also sent a signal of "implicit lower limit" for loan pricing this year, I take the average of the spread reductions from 2023 to 2024, that is, 4bp, and regard it as the impact of the spontaneous downward trend in the pricing of new loans on the spread in 2025.

3. On May 15, the reserve requirement ratio was cut by 0.5pct across the board, which is expected to improve the NIM by 1.2bp. This reserve requirement ratio cut is expected to provide about 1 trillion yuan of medium- and long-term liquidity. Assuming that the asset reallocation yield is the average yield of 3.67% of the interest-bearing assets of listed banks in 2024, the interest income will increase by about 36.7 billion yuan, boosting the NIM by 1.1bp. In addition, the liability side is expected to improve the NIM by 0.1bp. The reserve requirement ratio cut unfreezes the statutory reserves and converts them into excess reserves. Banks use excess reserves to replace other high-interest medium- and long-term liabilities, such as buy-out reverse repurchases and MLF. Considering that 900 billion yuan of 3M buy-out reverse repurchases and 125 billion yuan of MLF will expire in May, I assume that the liquidity released by the reserve requirement ratio cut will be used to replace buy-out reverse repurchases. The 3M buy-back reverse repurchase rate is basically in line with the rate of certificates of deposit with the same term. As of May 15, the central issuance rate of 3M interbank certificates of deposit was approximately 1.71%. Considering the statutory reserve rate of 1.62% and the excess reserve rate of 0.35%, this reserve requirement cut is expected to save 4.4 billion in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 0.1bp, which has a small impact.

4. OMO cuts interest rates by 10bp, and is expected to improve NIM by 1.3bp. Specifically: On the one hand, the cost of monetary policy tools is reduced. Considering the OMO rate cut, liquidity management tools such as MLF and buy-out reverse repurchase will also follow the rate cut and the magnitude will be roughly the same. As of May 9, the balance of OMO was 912.9 billion, and the MLF issued before the rate cut from May to December was due to mature at about 3.1 trillion, and the buy-out reverse repurchase was due to mature at about 4.6 trillion. Taking all factors into consideration, a 10bp rate cut is expected to save 8.6 billion in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 0.3bp. On the other hand, the cost of interbank liabilities is reduced. The interbank liability interest rate is 10bp lower than the policy interest rate, which mainly affects interbank certificates of deposit and interbank deposits. For interbank certificates of deposit, the maturity scale from June to December this year is about 15.7 trillion. For interbank deposits, the scale is approximately replaced by non- bank deposits, and the proportion of deposit cost reduction within the year refers to the current account ratio and fixed deposit ratio of listed banks within 6M in 2024, which is about 18.6 trillion. Overall, a 10bp reduction in interbank debt interest rates will save 34.3 billion in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 1bp.

5. The LPR rate cut of 10bp is expected to drag down the NIM by 3.1bp. On May 20, the 1-year LPR and the 5-year LPR were symmetrically lowered by 10bp. On the one hand, the interest rate of re-issued loans due to maturity has fallen. Since a considerable part of the impact of the 5-year LPR cut on the NIM occurs in the repricing of mortgage loans in 2026Q1. Therefore, the impact on the stock of loans this year is mainly reflected in non-housing loans. As of the end of April 2025, the scale of general loans excluding mortgage loans is about 213 trillion. Referring to the structure of the repricing or maturity period of listed bank loans at the end of 2024, the proportions of less than 3M/3M-1Y/1-5Y/5Y and above are 44%/47%/6%/2% respectively, of which the range involving repricing or maturity within the year is mainly 3M-1Y, so it is assumed that the proportion of re-issued loans due within the year is about 47%. Combined with the above assumptions, about 100 trillion existing loans will mature and be re-issued within the year, reducing interest income by 100.4 billion, resulting in a 3bp decline in NIM. On the other hand, the interest rate of incremental loans has declined. From June to December 2024, new general loans will be about 5.1 trillion. Considering the pre-emptive credit this year, subsequent demand may weaken. Therefore, it is assumed that the new scale in the same period this year will be 4.6 trillion, a year-on-year decrease of 500 billion. Therefore, the decline in the interest rate of newly issued loans is expected to reduce interest income by 4.6 billion, dragging down the NIM by 0.1bp, which has little impact.

6. The structural monetary policy tool cuts interest rates by 25bp, which is expected to improve the NIM by 0.4bp. As of the end of March 2025, the balance of structural monetary policy tools is 5.9 trillion yuan. A 25bp interest rate cut will save 14.8 billion yuan in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 0.4bp.

7. The interest rate of individual housing provident fund loans was reduced by 25bp, which is expected to drag down the NIM by 0.6bp. Generally speaking, the adjustment of the housing provident fund loan interest rate of the year will take effect on January 1 of the following year, so that borrowers and banks have sufficient time to adjust their repayment plans. Therefore, this policy mainly affects the NIM of banks in 2026. According to the latest data publicly stated by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development, the balance of housing provident fund loans at the end of 2023 was about 7.8 trillion yuan, an increase of nearly 7%. Assuming that the same growth rate is maintained in 2024, the loan balance will reach 8.3 trillion yuan at the end of 2024. Therefore, the interest rate cut of provident fund loans is expected to reduce interest income by 20.9 billion, dragging down the NIM by 0.6bp.

8. The reduction in the deposit interest rate in the second quarter is expected to improve the interest rate spread by about 3bp. On May 20, six state-owned banks, China Merchants Bank and Everbright Bank collectively lowered the RMB deposit interest rate. On May 21, seven joint-stock banks including Industrial Bank also followed suit. For most banks, demand deposits are reduced by 5bp, 3M-2Y regular deposits are reduced by 15bp, and 3Y-5Y are reduced by 25bp.For ease of calculation, I set this deposit rate cut to reduce demand deposits by 5bp and regular deposits by an average of 20bp. Referring to the maturity structure of listed bank deposits in 2024, demand/within 3M/3M-1Y/1-5Y and above account for approximately 39%/16%/21%/23% respectively. Among them, the main beneficiaries of this round of deposit rate cuts are demand deposits and 3M-1Y regular deposits that can be repriced at any time. As of the end of April 2025, the scale of demand deposits, time deposits and other deposits of financial institutions were 61.3 trillion and 176.4 trillion, respectively. Therefore, it is expected to save 105.7 billion in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 3.1bp.

9. High-interest long-term fixed-term deposits will be renewed at a reduced price upon maturity, and the interest rate spread is expected to improve by about 5bp. A large number of high-interest term deposits will mature in 2025, and the liability cost will be significantly improved after the price reduction and renewal. In recent years, the trend of regular and long-term deposits has gradually strengthened. In 2022 and 2023, the number of term deposits increased by 18.8 trillion and 20.7 trillion respectively, among which there are a large number of 1-5Y term deposits, and the average interest rate is basically above 2.5%. Therefore, a large number of 2-3Y term deposits absorbed in 2022-2023 will mature in 2025. Benefiting from the overall downward trend of deposit interest rates and the active guidance of banks to reduce the duration of deposits, whether it is renewal of deposits or replacement with interbank certificates of deposit, the liability cost is expected to be significantly improved. In 2022-2023, the number of term deposits and other deposits increased by 39.5 trillion. Assuming that 2-3Y deposits account for 50% of the new term deposits, the concentrated maturity scale in 2025 is about 19.7 trillion. Monitoring data from Rong360 Digital Technology Research Institute shows that the average interest rates of 2-year and 3-year bank deposits in March 2025 dropped by 87bp and 104bp respectively from September 2022 to 1.67% and 2.04%. The average issuance rate of 1Y interbank certificates of deposit this year is 1.90%, which is also much lower than the deposit rate at that time. Taking all factors into consideration, assuming that the average replacement cost decreases by 90bp, the high-interest term maturity price reduction will save 177.8 billion in interest expenses and improve the NIM by 5.2bp.

Combining the above estimates, I estimate that the decline in NIM for the whole year of 2025 will be around 10-11bp. Since the first quarter has already dropped by 10bp compared with last year, I expect the decline in the next three quarters to be very limited.

Limited room for China to cut interest rates in 2025

According to the calculations above, if Chinese commercial banks want to maintain the most basic sustainable operation in 2025, the interest margin bottom line is 0.67%. In addition, taking into account the known factors on both the asset and liability sides, I expect the NIM to drop by about 10-11 bp to 1.41%-1.42% in 2025, corresponding to an interest margin safety cushion of 74-75bp.

However, the marginal increase in the interest rate safety cushion does not mean that the deposit and loan interest rates will fall more in 2025. First, the recent monetary policy will focus on the health of the banking system. In recent years, the monetary policy implementation report has twice explicitly focused on the operating pressure of banks, especially the interest rate pressure. Overall, after experiencing the rapid narrowing of the interest rate safety cushion and the intensification of the idle capital phenomenon, the central bank has paid more attention to "maintaining the health of the banking system" when formulating monetary policy. Therefore, in order to maintain the interest rate safety cushion at a satisfactory level, the central bank may not guide the policy interest rate to fall sharply, which is also the reason why the 7-day OMO interest rate and LPR in the package of monetary policies were only lowered by 10bp.

Secondly, lowering the scale requirements for credit issuance is also intended to protect the stability of interest rate spreads. Except for the period when monetary policy returns to normal in 2021, the decline in the weighted average interest rate of corporate loans since 2020 has been significantly higher than the decline in LPR. However, this phenomenon changed in 2024, with the weighted average interest rate of corporate loans falling by 41bp, far lower than the 60bp decline in the 5-year LPR. This shows that when pricing loans in 2024, the average reduction in LPR points may be smaller than in previous years. In 2024, the central bank repeatedly emphasized the weakening of the "scale complex" of credit issuance. One of the important starting points is to avoid the "involution" of the industry leading to excessive downward movement of loan interest rates, which in turn triggers capital idling and arbitrage. It is expected that the central bank will continue this attitude in 2025 and appropriately reduce the requirements for the scale of credit issuance to maintain the stability of bank interest rate spreads.

Third, the central bank strengthened self-regulatory supervision of deposit and loan interest rate pricing to protect bank interest rate spreads. On the deposit side, the manual interest supplement rectification in April 2024 focused on solving the problem of "open reduction and hidden increase" in public deposit interest rates caused by illegal bypassing of internal pricing authorization. The interbank self-regulatory initiative in November 2024 set the upper limit of non-bank interbank demand interest rates at the 7-day reverse repurchase rate, with some reductions as high as 30bp. Enterprises and non-bank institutions have strong bargaining power, and self-regulatory rectification effectively constrains these two deposit interest rate highlands. On the loan side, Column 3 of the 2024Q3 Monetary Policy Implementation Report proposed " urging banks to follow the risk pricing principle and not to issue loans with after-tax interest rates lower than the yield of treasury bonds of the same term." Consumer loans with interest rates below 3% were also stopped from April this year. On the eve of the LPR reduction in May, the lower limit of the interest rate for the first home loan in Guangzhou was also raised by 10bp to 3.1%. Judging from the regulatory statements and the business behavior of commercial banks, there may still be an implicit lower limit for loan pricing, which is also to prevent abnormally low loan interest rates from increasing bank interest rate spread pressure.

Judging from the relatively restrained decline in policy interest rates and LPR, the reduction in requirements for credit scale, and the strengthening of supervision on interest rate pricing, the central bank intends to solve the phenomenon of excessive downward trend in interest rate spreads caused by "loan interest rates falling quickly" and "deposit interest rates unable to fall." This year, the central bank may continue this regulatory approach to maintain the health of interest rate spreads, so it is expected that the decline in deposit and loan interest rates this year will not be too large.

data.title: Thornburg International Equity Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Let’s acknowledge the shared challenges faced by both portfolio managers and clients as we navigate today’s volatile market landscape. With headlines dominated by inflation concerns, recession fears, and tariff tensions, it is no surprise investors are feeling uneasy. Uncertainty causes doubt. Yet, it is precisely in these moments of uncertainty that opportunities emerge, especially in international markets.

For the past decade and a half, U.S. equities have taken center stage. But now, international markets—often underappreciated—are revealing compelling potential. As the U.S. contends with persistent inflation, China faces deflation and plans a massive stimulus, Japan moves beyond a decades-long deflationary environment, and Europe begins to assert greater economic independence, international markets offer a diverse set of investment conditions worth exploring.

While tariffs remain a topical issue for the U.S., they are far less of a focus among other regions. It’s important not to project the U.S. experience onto other economies—each operates under its own unique set of circumstances.

Market swings can be unsettling, but volatility is not inherently negative. For long-term investors, it can be a powerful ally. Volatility creates windows of opportunity—periods when patience, discipline, and a long-term mindset can pave the way for positive outcomes. During the recent bouts of market turbulence, We have been actively capitalizing on select opportunities.

Every economic cloud has a silver lining. Our team is here to help our clients navigate through uncertain waters—with perspective, conviction, a steady hand, and some grey hairs, too.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Based on our fundamental, stock-focused research, we continue to see compelling prospects in select, high-quality industrial, financial and information technology stocks that have competitive advantages in their respective geographies, and in some cases, globally. These include companies in Japan, France and Germany, among other jurisdictions. We also have meaningful allocations to attractively valued opportunities among utilities and health care, given their strong fundamentals and earnings profiles.

The Trump administration telegraphed its intention to employ tariffs to rebalance global trade long ago. Our active positioning has enabled us to anticipate and navigate a variety of complex environments effectively. It is a basic part of our risk management process.

We have strategically managed tariff-related risks, intentionally avoiding excessive exposure to companies whose business models depend heavily on factors beyond their control, particularly shipping products into the U.S.

We invest from the bottom up but are always cognizant of the operating environments of our portfolio holdings. We are aware of macro, foreign exchange, and geopolitical dynamics, including tariff and trade-war risks. But we believe it is most effective to focus on the nature, durability and visibility of earnings, along with rigorous valuation analysis and, at times, cost-effective FX hedges. This is how our strategies have negotiated general market volatility over many years.

This high-conviction, yet diversified portfolio aims to deliver superior risk-adjusted returns through market and economic cycles by participating in market upswings, as we saw in the first half of 2024, as well as by protecting in the downturns, such as in the first quarter of 2025. We appreciate that broad market swings often cause share prices to deviate sharply from our assessment of business fundamentals and intrinsic value. We are always ready to take advantage of opportunities as they arise.

Our focus on a concentrated set of diverse international stocks that demonstrate high-quality management, solid fundamentals and a clear path to value realization, has served us well both in turbulent and fair-weather conditions, in the short and long term. This flexible and, we find, repeatable process should help us navigate the unpredictable policy and economic volatility in 2025.

data.title: EWU: UK Stocks Soaring In 2025, Decade-Plus Highs In Sight
data.description:
International stocks have made quite a comeback in 2025. The Vanguard FTSE All-World Ex-US ETF (VEU), the fund I use to track the performance of foreign shares, is up 15% year-to-date, dividends included. The S&P 500 ETF (SPY), by contrast, is higher by just 2%.

Outperforming VEU is the iShares MSCI United Kingdom ETF (NYSEARCA:EWU). I had a buy rating on the fund six months ago, and EWU is up 18% since then, beating the S&P 500 by 17 percentage points.

But global equities are not as much of a bargain today. According to Goldman Sachs, as illustrated below, many international markets now trade above the long-term average valuation. Still, I reiterate a buy rating on EWU. I see the valuation as reasonable, while new momentum in the fund could take it to its 2014 highs in the mid-$40s.

European Equities Remain Cheap, But Their P/E Has Risen

UK Leads on the Total Shareholder Yield View

EWU +19% in 2025, Strong Among the 45 Country ETFs Seeking Alpha Tracks

According to the issuer, EWU seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of UK equities. It offers investors targeted exposure to large and mid-sized companies in the UK and is used to express a country view. The fund invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization. It seeks to track the performance of the MSCI United Kingdom Index, by using a representative sampling technique.

Despite sharp YTD alpha with EWU versus the S&P 500, it remains a small to moderate-sized ETF with just $3.1 billion in assets under management as of June 2, 2025. That’s just marginally bigger than the fund was at the time of my Q4 2024 analysis—yet another reason to be upbeat on the product, since it’s not as if investors are overly bullish or euphoric on EWU.

Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is more than two percentage points above that of the SPX at 3.5%. Pictured above, the total shareholder yield with UK stocks is about the highest you’ll find around the world.

Share-price momentum has gone from lousy to lustrous—the ETF Grade in that category per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system is now a strong A. Risk levels are in check, too, despite intense volatility in March and April. Finally, EWU is highly liquid, given an average daily volume of almost two million shares and a median 30-day bid/ask spread of only three basis points, per iShares.

For an update on the portfolio, the UK ETF plots along the top row of the Morningstar style box, indicating its high exposure to large caps. That helps to keep the quality of the allocation high, but it also means there isn’t much in the way of size diversification. I’d also call out that ex-US small caps have performed very well this year.

For EWU’s valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is low at 13.5, while the long-term EPS growth rate is moderate at 8.92%. That yields a PEG ratio of 1.50x. Compared to my previous analysis, the PEG has jumped a quarter point, which is significant.

EWU: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

From a sector view, EWU’s portfolio looks much different from that of the S&P 500. Financials is the largest weight at 22%, and Information Technology, which is more than 30% of the SPX, is less than a percent of the ETF. So, if we see cyclical and defensives outperform, with the Mag 7 US mega caps lagging, then EWU should continue to produce alpha.

Of course, many hinges on how the US Dollar Index (DXY) performs. Through early June, the DXY is down 8.5%. The EUR-USD pair has soared by 9.1%. That works to the benefit of investors in EWU since the fund is priced in dollars.

EWU: Holdings & Dividend Information

Unfortunately, not everything comes up roses for UK equities. Over the past 10 years of price data, June has been the worst month on the calendar. Moreover, the August through October stretch has been bearish, too. So, aside from solid July price action, seasonality is a headwind.

EWU: Bearish June Through October Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With shares still attractive on valuation, but no longer dirt-cheap, the fund has a very bullish chart. Notice in the graph below that EWU rallied through the key $38 level that I pointed out last time. With the upside move, a measured advance to between $43 and $44 is now in play, based on the height of the multi-quarter trading range. What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is decidedly positively sloped, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart—it has been hovering in a bullish spot, though it’s close to technical overbought conditions. With a high amount of volume by price below today’s level, there should be ample cushion if we see a protracted retreat.

If we were to zoom out the view to its entire history, we’d find that the 2014 high of $44 could easily be struck this year. By the way, $55 is the all-time peak from 2007.

EWU: Bullish Upside Breakout Targets the mid-$40s, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on EWU. The fund has soared since my previous analysis in December 2024, and I still see possible upside given the modest valuation, high momentum, and positive technicals.

data.title: Despite Tourism Risks, Trip.Com Strikes Me As A Good Buy
data.description:
Right off the bat, I have to acknowledge that I am a bit wary of the tourism sector in general, as high inflation and the ongoing trade war could eventually impact travel and leisure. Given that holidays are often planned months in advance, I believe this summer’s holiday season will be fine, but we may see cooling growth and spending come autumn, and especially in 2026.

That said, the Trip.com Group (NASDAQ:TCOM) strikes me as an attractive option, enjoying strong growth, and various tailwinds could potentially propel the company upward. My current worries over the tourism industry have made me hesitant to pull the trigger, but I do believe TCOM is worthy of a buy designation and may end up adding a long stake despite my trepidations.

Anchored in China, Expanding Globally

TCOM is actually the largest online travel agency in China and is headquartered in Shanghai. China’s economy has been a mixed bag as of late, but in some ways, that could offer some benefits for TCOM as many Chinese are shifting away from expensive overseas vacations and instead sticking closer to home, where TCOM is currently at its strongest. In 2024, domestic trips grew by nearly 15% to top 5.6 billion in China.

Outbound Chinese tourism has also been increasing rapidly since the end of strict COVID-19 protocols, yet as of 2024, outbound tourism has yet to fully recover from pre-COVID times. Projections currently peg domestic Chinese tourism spending to reach a record-breaking $968 billion this year, which bodes well for TCOM.

Another thing to consider is that Chinese households are sitting on massive savings. While the real estate crisis has hurt many Chinese households, savings remain high. Total household deposits, according to Reuters, exceed $22 trillion USD and World Bank data shows gross domestic savings regularly exceeding 40% of China's GDP. The Chinese government has been moving to suppress interest rates paid on deposits to spur spending, although so far, Chinese consumers remain reticent. If the Chinese government can figure out how to get Chinese citizens to spend, it’d bode well for TCOM.

That said, while the Chinese market offers a good foundation for TCOM, the company’s long-term goal is to expand international operations. Outbound Chinese tourism could help with that, and since international travel often results in higher budgets and more spending, it could drive more revenues and profits for TCOM.

For now, TCOM is emphasizing the Asia Pacific market according to statements from company leadership during the latest earnings call. Within the APAC region, the biggest direct competitor is probably Agoda, which is owned by Booking (NYSE:BKNG). Agoda has led the Asian market for years, and BKNG is no slouch, yet curiously, Agoda gets hammered when it comes to customer reviews. TCOM, on the other hand, performs much better (more on this later).

Anecdotally, I plugged in some July travel dates on both platforms for Kuala Lumpur, Bangkok, Hong Kong, and Tokyo. Prices and selection for both seemed competitive and on par with one another. Prices were also in line with what I saw on Airbnb. Granted, this particular analysis was far from scientific and was simply a quick glance.

AI and Experiences: A Two-Headed Growth Dragon?

TCOM’s leadership made sure to emphasize AI during their earnings call. While AI has become a bit of a buzzword in some ways, I do believe it could prove especially disruptive for client journeys that offer lots of upselling opportunities, including vacation and travel planning. I believe AI could be used to examine individual customer journeys and then tweak ads and promotional offers in a much more minute way than traditional mass data analysis.

Customer service is also crucial, but likely expensive for travel agencies like TCOM. Problems with bookings, basic questions, payment issues, and much more could quickly lead to high customer service expenses. I believe that travel companies like TCOM may ultimately stand to disproportionately benefit from AI agents and other services.

Another potential area for growth is in selling “experiences.” I’m not sure how much upside is here, to be honest, but Airbnb seems to have enjoyed success, and experiences at popular destinations have garnered hundreds and even thousands of reviews on Airbnb. TCOM may find similar traction. If nothing else, the company is demonstrating that it’s flexible and willing to try new ideas.

Customer Satisfaction Should Drive Adoption

Keeping customers happy can be especially difficult in the tourism industry. Unhappy customers are often the loudest customers, leaving negative reviews and expressing discontent on social media. Head to TrustPilot and Agoda scores just 1.2 out of 5, while Expedia (NYSE:EXPE) reels in the same 1.2 out of 5 score. Meanwhile, Trip.com scores 4.4 out of 5.

On TrustPilot, Agoda and Expedia are frequently accused of poor customer support and troubles with bookings. While you can certainly find unhappy customers for Trip.com, most reviews laud the company for effective customer service, easy check-ins, and savings. If Trip.com can continue to keep customers happy while also saving them money, the company should be able to snag market share from Agoda and Expedia.

TCOM Valuation and Fundamentals

It can be hard these days to find a company at a (historically) good valuation. P/E ratios have been trending up, and many investments, even in blue chips, have become at least somewhat speculative. This actually makes TCOM rather attractive with its P/E GAAP (TTM) of ~18.5, which is inline with the sector median.

Using Gurufocus’s DCF calculator to pin down a reverse DCF (with the discount rate set at 10% and the terminal growth set at 4%), free cash flows will need to grow by ~36% to justify the company’s current valuation. However, when we look at an EPS calculation with the same calculator, EPS growth will need to weigh in at just 9%. Given that the company is still in an early international expansionary stage, I believe free cash flows and EPS will trend upward.

Profitability is quite high with the gross profit margin topping 81% and the net income margin weighing in at 30.84%, according to Seeking Alpha's data. Year-over-year revenue growth is more moderate at 16.95%, but with expansion efforts underway, this could accelerate. EPS Diluted Growth (YoY) is also excellent at 51.63%. All in all, TCOM strikes me fundamentally as a good company.

My Reservations With Tourism/Leisure In General

Following the COVID-19 pandemic, travel surged globally. Part of this was due to pent-up demand and people looking to get outside of the homes they had, in some cases, been practically trapped in for months on end. The pandemic also resulted in high savings levels, which made it easier for people to afford vacations. Yet those savings have since run out.

Now, we’re seeing rising credit card debt, suggesting that consumers are turning to their credit cards to sustain their lifestyles. At the same time, credit card delinquencies have begun to tick upward. While not a crisis for now, both rising credit card debt and delinquencies could hint at future belt-tightening, especially if the unemployment rate should rise or household earnings stall or shrink.

Inflation could also pinch household budgets. While inflation has actually cooled in recent months, the ongoing global trade war could spur inflation in the not-so-distant future.

Even if consumers do start to tighten their belts, we likely won’t see the full effects for several months. Vacations are often planned months in advance, and many travelers have already paid for their trips. That said, I do believe tourism/leisure will be among the first things put on the chopping block if the economic environment deteriorates.

My Take-Away: TCOM is a Soft Buy, But Keep an Eye on Consumer Spending and Tourism

As a company, I believe Trip.com has plenty of room to grow, and positive customer experiences should propel success. Agoda/Booking.com and Expedia both strike me as vulnerable companies owing to negative customer reviews and minimal moats. While Trip.com currently seems focused on expanding in Asia for now, future pushes into other markets could spur further growth.

That said, I do have concerns over tourism and leisure in general. More people are living off credit cards, and delinquencies are on the rise. The ongoing trade war could further spur inflation and weigh down on consumer sentiments. This may result in would-be travelers skipping vacations in the future. While the 2025 summer will likely see high travel numbers overall, it’s important to remember that many vacations are booked months in advance. If consumers do tighten their belts, we may not see the full effects until late 2025 and into 2026.

If I do go long TCOM, I will keep a close eye on consumer spending and might make a quick exit if I find evidence that spending is coming under serious pressure.

data.title: Palantir: I'm Not Playing The FOMO Casino [Rating Upgrade]
data.description:
Investment thesis

I continue to believe that Palantir’s (NASDAQ:PLTR) fundamentals are stellar. However, several strong indications suggest that its valuation is not connected to fundamentals or any reasonable calculations. I have simulated various [even insane] FCF growth scenarios but none of them justify PLTR’s 300 billion market cap. At the same time, my previous bearish article looks not good as PLTR rallied by 50%+ since it was posted. This bubble keeps inflated by the FOMO, which makes timing the reversal almost impossible, and I do not want to be part of this casino. Therefore, I upgrade PLTR to “Hold” and will wait for a deep pullback from safe distance.

Recent developments

Palantir released its Q1 earnings on May 5, beating consensus estimates both from the revenue and EPS perspective. Palantir's quarterly YoY revenue growth accelerated to an impressive 39.3% from around 36% delivered in Q4 2024. Bottom line growth followed the impressive revenue dynamic as the company's EPS increased from $0.08 to $0.13.

It was high-quality EPS growth as it was achieved thanks to the operating margin expansion. The Q1 operating margin expanded from 12.8% to 19.9% on a YoY basis, which indicates strong operating leverage. This is a crucial bullish factor as it means that Palantir's FCF generation capacity is improving. As a result, PLTR's balance sheet becomes stronger and stronger every quarter. As of the latest reportable date, PLTR had $5.4 billion in cash with almost no debt. Thus, the company is extremely well positioned to invest aggressively in future growth.

The upcoming quarterly earnings release is scheduled for August 4. The Q2 revenue is forecasted by consensus to be $939 million, which will be 38.5% higher if compared to the same quarter of FY2024. The operating leverage improvement is expected to remain impressive as the EPS is forecasted to show a 53.8% YoY growth.

The notable indication that we might evidence peak revenue growth of Palantir is the fact that Wall Street analysts expect quarters after Q2 FY2025 to demonstrate consistent revenue growth rate decline through the end of FY2026. Revenue growth deceleration is rarely absorbed by investors with optimism, which is an indication that it is the peak PLTR optimism at the moment.

The fact that insiders continue selling the stock quite aggressively is another warning sign. In the summary below we can see that there are frequent sales, including the ones from the highest level executives like Alex Karp and Shyam Sankar. Such aggressive insider selling is rarely a bullish sign. It is highly likely that insiders might consider current valuation as extremely attractive for selling, not buying.

PLTR's technical setup is also far from being called attractive with its 65.5 RSI indicator. This is not far below the overbought zone and is also notably higher compared to the average level. A relatively high RSI indicator also means that expectations of the market are running hot. Thus, buying PLTR at the moment is quite risky from the technical analysis perspective as well.

Overall, the picture looks mixed. On the one hand, there is impressive revenue growth coupled with robust operating leverage. On the other hand, the next few quarters are expected to deliver revenue growth deceleration, which means that the current revenue growth rate has peaked. Quite high RSI indicator and aggressive insider selling also point to extremely high expectations of the stock market.

Valuation update

The "PLTR 5Y Avg." column in the table below suggests that Palantir's valuation ratios have been historically sky-high. However, current multiples are way above even historically high valuation ratios. Palantir's forward GAAP P/E ratio is 286, which looks absolutely unrealistic and out of this world. To illustrate, Cisco's P/E ratio peaked at 201 in March 2000 amid the dot-com bubble. Therefore, valuation ratios indicate that Palantir the "bubble of all bubbles".

Using even extremely aggressive growth assumptions for DCF analysis also does not justify PLTR's $300+ billion market cap. I have simulated various aggressive growth scenarios, and all of them suggested that the company's fair value is way below $300 billion. But let's go step-by-step and begin with the discount rate explanation. There is almost no debt in Palantir's capital structure, making cost of equity the most suitable to calculate the company's NPV. The cost of equity is 15.6% [rounded].

To make my DCF model more optimistic, this time I use unadjusted FCF margin, meaning that I ignore Palantir's significant stock-based compensation [SBC]. Therefore, the first-year FCF margin assumption is 30%. Due to PLTR's robust operating leverage, the metric is expected to expand by 200-bps per year. The FY2026 revenue assumption is $5.01 billion. The base-case scenario incorporates a 35.9% revenue CAGR with a very aggressive 6% perpetual growth rate.

The above DCF model is extremely aggressive, just look at it. Revenue is expected to increase by the factor of 6+ over the next seven years and the FCF margin to achieve 42%, a level not demonstrated even by well-known FCF champions like Meta or Google. Nevertheless, DCF suggests that Palantir's fair value is $162 billion, almost twice as lower as the current market cap.

The crazy part is that the company's fair value remains below $300 billion even if unrealistic 50% revenue CAGR is incorporated for the next seven years.

Risks update

I have to confess that I have not been very strong in timing PLTR’s ups and downs. I am not boastful, but I know that I had many extremely good and timely calls on other tickers, but PLTR is not one of them. Therefore, it might be a poor timing again and we are very close to a big PLTR selloff while I am upgrading the stock’s rating to “Hold”.

We see that growth expectations of investors are extremely high, which means that the Q2 earnings season might be a negative catalyst if PLTR fails to deliver on expectations or provide an upbeat guidance. Therefore, the reversal point might be approaching, and I might overlook it.

On the other hand, I might be wrong that I am not supporting the FOMO-driven thesis because there are indications that PLTR’s non-fundamental bull run might continue for a while as seasonality patterns suggest that June-July are historically very strong months.

Conclusion

To conclude, I upgraded PLTR to "Hold" despite vast overvaluation because choosing a side at this moment looks extremely risky. The FOMO-backed rally might continue, which is supported by robust historical seasonality patterns. On the other hand, with such a massive overvaluation, even a slightly unfavorable headline might cause a big PLTR sell-off.

data.title: Healthcare Tech And Innovation: Heart Monitors Improving
data.description:
Healthcare and technology innovation has far-reaching impacts that can truly transform and save lives. One area of ongoing focus is the growing challenge posed by heart disease. Heart disease is the leading cause of death in the U.S. Someone dies from cardiovascular disease every 33 seconds. These conditions encompass ailments like stroke, heart failure and, notably, atrial fibrillation (AFib). The latter is a rapidly growing issue, projected to affect up to 12 million Americans by 2030.

Diagnostics are vital to detecting heart problems, but traditional cardiac monitoring has had limitations. This note discusses the important advancements of the iRhythm (IRTC) Zio Monitor. iRhythm is a constituent of the Robo Global Healthcare Technology and Innovation Index (HTEC). This index identifies disrupting and emerging technologies in the healthcare sector.

Traditional Cardiac Monitoring Limitations

Many became aware of AFib through smartwatches and their EKG features. Yet, clinical diagnosis traditionally relied on methods like Holter monitors, event monitors, and 12-lead ECGs. These tools offered a high level of accuracy. But they often presented challenges regarding patient comfort and the limited duration of monitoring they covered.

For instance, Holter monitors, resembling an early iPod with five wires connected to the body, are typically worn for only 24-48 hours. This not only makes them uncomfortable but also means they might miss sporadic heart events. Event monitors, generally prescribed for 30 days, share similar discomfort issues and require patients to activate them upon detecting a symptom. Standard ECGs, while highly accurate, are performed in-clinic, meaning they can usually only diagnose an arrhythmia if it occurs during that specific visit.

Typical application of different methods of cardiac monitoring. From left to right: ECG, Holter Monitor and Zio Monitor.

A Modern Solution: Rhythm Zio Monitor

Recognizing this unmet need, back in 2009, iRhythm introduced the Zio long-term cardiac monitor (LTCM). The Zio monitor is engineered for up to 14 days of use, requires no obtrusive leads, and is secured to the patient via a simple patch, while at the same time improving performance. The monitor significantly aids in the earlier detection and management of heart rhythm problems.

Today, iRhythm’s Next Generation Zio Monitor is its smallest, lightest, and thinnest device yet. It incorporates a pioneering deep-learned algorithm, clinically proven to be as accurate as cardiologists in detecting 13 types of arrhythmia classes, along with specific beat types and runs.

Clinical Studies Highlight Zio’s Effectiveness

The CAMELOT study, published last year in the American Heart Journal, underscored the benefits of this approach. It revealed that for every 1,000 patients monitored with standard Holter monitors, there was an association with 180 more emergency department visits, 80 additional hospitalizations, and 920 more outpatient visits, when compared to those who used the Zio LTCM service. In other words, the Zio monitor’s earlier, more accurate diagnoses significantly reduced the need for subsequent medical interventions.

Building on this, iRhythm published the AVALON study in April of this year. This landmark real-world comparative evaluation involved 428,770 commercially insured patients with no previous cardiac monitoring history. The findings were compelling. Patients using the Zio LTCM service demonstrated superior outcomes across new arrhythmia diagnoses, reduced need for retesting, and fewer cardiovascular events compared to other ambulatory cardiac monitoring methods.

New Arrhythmia Diagnosis: Baseline Percentages (within 90 days)

The study population distribution where 36% of patients used LTCM, 36% used a Holter monitor, and 27% used an ambulatory event monitor (AEM). “Non-iRhythm LTCM” refers to other long-term cardiac monitoring services, which in the broader study included Bardy LTCM, Biotelemetry LTCM, Preventice LTCM, and “Other LTCM” providers.

Source: Comparative effectiveness of a novel P-wave-centric artificial intelligence algorithm with extended=wear Holter for new arrhythmia diagnosis: The AVALON study. American Heart Journal, 262, 101–111

As the table indicates, the study found that Zio LTCM users were more likely to secure a new arrhythmia diagnosis than individuals using alternative monitoring devices. In short, the Zio LTCM proved more effective at identifying cardiac issues. Compellingly, Zio LTCM users were also 1.95 times less likely to need retesting within 180 days when compared to those employing other non-iRhythm LTCMs.

Zio LTCM Performance Compared to Other Modalities

Zio LTCM Comparative Effectiveness for Arrhythmia Diagnosis, Retesting, and Cardiovascular Events. Likelihood ratios indicate how much more or less likely Zio LTCM is to result in these outcomes compared to specified monitoring modalities.

Source: Comparative effectiveness of a novel P-wave-centric artificial intelligence algorithm with extended-wear Holter for new arrhythmia diagnosis: The AVALON study. American Heart Journal, 262, 101–111

Addressing Gaps in Cardiac Care Access

These improved diagnostic capabilities are particularly relevant in addressing “cardiology deserts.” These are areas where patient access to specialized cardiac care is severely limited. Over 46% of all U.S. counties, home to 22 million residents, lack a practicing cardiologist. These regions are typically more rural, face socioeconomic disadvantages, and bear a higher burden of cardiovascular disease. This is exacerbated by the fact that AFib is estimated to be up to three times more prevalent than previously understood, affecting at least 10.55 million American adults.

iRhythm’s Growth & Global Expansion

With an established U.S. market penetration of over 30%, iRhythm is now extending its reach internationally. In May of this year, the company announced its launch in Japan, a country where 1.6 million ambulatory cardiac monitoring tests are performed annually. iRhythm is targeting further market opportunities totaling over 5 million tests across EU and APAC countries.

iRhythm’s innovative contributions are recognized by its inclusion in the ROBO Global Healthcare Technology and Innovation Index (HTEC). This index showcases a diversified portfolio of global companies at the forefront of disruptive and emerging technologies in healthcare. The Zio system shares this platform with other leading-edge technologies, including surgical robots, blood-based cancer testing, lab-grown organs, gene-editing therapies and many more ground-breaking advances in health tech.

HTEC is the underlying index for the Robo Global Healthcare Technology & Innovation ETF (HTEC).

It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.
